chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Conference abstracts and reporting guideline.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Conference abstracts and reporting guideline.docx
text:
Guidelines for Conference Abstracts and reporting
General considerations
You are encouraged to go and present your work in at least one conference per year. 
It is encouraged to submit intermediate results or plans and not complete final results. The basic idea is that you submit a project and then present whatever is ready at the time of the meeting.
The lab can pay for your attendance to one conference per year on the conditions that 
You submit an abstract for a poster/talk
You have applied for a travel grant and been declined the grant, OR you were ineligible to apply for travel grants suitable for the particular conference.
-> Check also the travel grant instructions. 
Guidelines for the abstract
Plan your submission
Select conference and submission deadlines
Communicate your plans to your project team 
Let everyone in the project know your plans as soon as you know eg. send a message to the project channel. This is to avoid potential overlap if others also want to submit to the same conference.
Coordinate your timeframe with the project - e.g. when do you anticipate to have
Communicate with team members (including PIs/Anniina) when you plan to send it to them for comments 
Write the Abstract 
Check the technical instructions for the abstract (length, structure, requirements)
Align your title and writing to the scope of the meeting. Eg. if more technical/methodological or biological discovery
Include co-authors based on who have contributed to the project. 
Check and add author affiliations from previous abstracts and https://docs.google.com/document/d/1xb9KZoMqUHc3L5TDfdA92wPMh_I7pE3sL6ZAUWurMMc/edit?usp=sharing
Even if abstract is not structured, use a structured way to write it: 
Background and motivation: 2-3 sentences
Methods (1/4 of the length)

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Conference abstracts and reporting guideline.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Conference abstracts and reporting guideline.docx
text:
stracts and https://docs.google.com/document/d/1xb9KZoMqUHc3L5TDfdA92wPMh_I7pE3sL6ZAUWurMMc/edit?usp=sharing
Even if abstract is not structured, use a structured way to write it: 
Background and motivation: 2-3 sentences
Methods (1/4 of the length) 
Results ½ of length - so most of text should be dedicated to Results. If you dont have enough - describe the cohort, samples, methodology. You can also what you plan to do by the time of the conference
Conclusions 2-3 sentences
Tip! It is difficult to write abstracts if you are in the beginning or in the middle of your data analysis. However, to go to a conference the abstract reviewers need to see that you have results - otherwise the abstract is too preliminary. The tip how to do this is to use descriptive results! Describe the data you have, describe e.g. cell phenotypes, cell numbers etc. Did you observe heterogeneity? Did you see something interesting in the data? How were the data distributed in your cohort? What was the most common cell type/neighbourhood/feature that you observed? All results dont need to be statistically significant, robust validated scientific findings. 
Share abstract for comments
Store your abstract in the Conference abstracts and materials folder - make a sub-folder for the Conference if not already existing. https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ayp0s-3Q6CUC6lMVUm4N6JgDudg2k8Ok?usp=drive_link
First share to the project PIs (Anniina)
Confirm availability to check before, then give 1-2 days to comment and suggest a date as the deadline for comments. 
Use editable online platform eg. google or sharepoint - remember to share edit/comment permissions
Give a deadline when you need the comments

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Conference abstracts and reporting guideline.docx::chunk_0002
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Conference abstracts and reporting guideline.docx
text:
ilability to check before, then give 1-2 days to comment and suggest a date as the deadline for comments. 
Use editable online platform eg. google or sharepoint - remember to share edit/comment permissions
Give a deadline when you need the comments 
After PI comments, share to all co-authors via email and #abstracts channel minimum 3 days before submission
Request to confirm affiliations and if they have any comments 
After conference material submission and reporting
To ensure transparency and impact of attending conferences 
After your conference, save your abstract and poster/presentation in Conference abstracts and materials 
Make one subfolder per conference (i.e. “EACR 2025 Lisbon”), and store your abstract + poster/presentation in the relevant folder. Please name the files with your name and the conference name + year for easier backtracking of conference attendances/presentations
At the conference make notes of key points to prepare a summary of the key presentations/findings in the conference and upload those to the same folder with your conference materials
Present your conference summary (max 10 min) in a suitable slot during a lab meeting - contact Joonas about booking the slot. Potential slots (with only 1 TRC/project presentation slotted to the lab meeting) can be found from the lab meeting schedule (Lab Meetings 2026)

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to give a great presentation.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to give a great presentation.docx
text:
When planning a presentation there are some general things to consider
1. How long is your talk? - It is of 1st priority to stick to the allocated time. The shorter the presentation - the better it has to be and and more effort you need to put in preparing it!
2. What is your audience? - Think of who will be in the audience, and what is their background? For ovarian cancer researchers you dont need to explain ovarian cancer. 
3. Add the correct Acknowledgements! This is of utmost importance - acknowledge everyone who have contributed and enabled your work, including the funding agencies. 
4. Practice, practice, practice - ask friends, family members to listen to you present. Organise at least 1-2 rehearsal talks! 
When you are making the presentation
1. Focus on LOGIC
Every slide should have one main message = title in a short sentence. Titles for "results" slides are either the main RESULT or CONCLUSION of data presented on your slide. 
In general: 1-2 introductory slides, 80% of your slides should be on results, and 1-2 conclusion slides. 
2. Focus on CLEARNESS
Use minimum amount of text/words - words instead of sentences
Avoid too busy slides - max 2 images per slide - > split slides, use animations
Every image and animation on your slides should have a reason to exist
3. Get feedback!
Send your slides to your project members, peers, and supervisor for comments.
Plan what to say and write down the script of your talk
1. Focus on LOGIC and CLEARNESS
• Think of 1-3 main messages you wish to convey for each slide
• Formulate these to 2-6 complete sentences – write them to the notes
• Sentences should be short and logical –avoid complicated words
2. Focus on MEANING
• What does this mean? Interpret your results and graphs - use whole sentences.

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to give a great presentation.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to give a great presentation.docx
text:
or each slide
• Formulate these to 2-6 complete sentences – write them to the notes
• Sentences should be short and logical –avoid complicated words
2. Focus on MEANING
• What does this mean? Interpret your results and graphs - use whole sentences. 
• Add 1-2 explanatory sentences to your main result slides
3. Focus on TRANSITIONS
• Think how and why each topic leads to the next step, and say this in your talk.

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx
text:
Here are some tips about how to make a nice poster according to my experience
"Conference attendees will look at your poster only briefly, so a clear presentation is crucial" [2]
Firstly, planning is critical! Leave enough time to prepare your poster. The first time - it will take longer than you expected.
Plan the schedule:
1) Check where and when you will send it for printing
2) Leave at least 1 week for feedback and edits. Check with Anniina and potentially your other project members and collaborators when they can give comments to the poster.
This is also critical to make sure to present the correct affiliations, external collaborators and funding. 
3) When you are done - A good tip - ask your friend, colleague to do a final grammarly/overall check for mistakes before printing.
After you have finished the poster - save and share the abstract and poster in datacloud: https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/f/1018895
Name your poster and abstract files: Project_Yourlastname_Conference_Date in the format of NKI_Perez_AACROvarian2023_20231002
1. Choose a nice software to make the poster
I recommend inkscape: https://inkscape.org/
Pros:
It allows you to make SVG files.
All the objects never change/move, are just the way you add them.
It has hundreds of options available, like how to adjust colors, align all text.
Once you know how to use it, it saves you so much time.
Cons: 
It takes so much time to learn how to use it
It is a bit complex to use, and not intuitive as power-point.
The second I recommend is Microsoft Publisher.
I have done all my posters in Microsof PowerPoint (@aemoksan@helsinki_fi )
It has all the necessary features and is easy to use
Be careful when you import images from other software- they may loose resolution especially if you copy-paste.

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx
text:
Publisher.
I have done all my posters in Microsof PowerPoint (@aemoksan@helsinki_fi )
It has all the necessary features and is easy to use
Be careful when you import images from other software- they may loose resolution especially if you copy-paste. Paste special as either tiff or pdf usually overcomes this issue.
I do not recommend: Microsoft Word, Biorender, because all the text and figures are always moving whenever you are editing any section. 
2. Identify a nice template or poster structure
Check other posters made by the Lab-Members you can find those in the G-Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1CJ_cpKOdLEwb4cv0lDGr1yLKBEkq5eMN?usp=share_link
and in the Datacloud: https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/apps/files/?dir=/farkkila/Presentations_posters_abstracts&fileid=1018895
Check the further reading materials to get to know nice poster structures.
3. Text and font
Make sure to not add so much text, only the indispensable one.
For your figures, make sure the legends and axis-names are large enough to be seen far away.
For the methods, if possible create a figure or couple of figures describing the methods instead of adding text.
4. Your Figures
Your poster must contain mostly figures. Add the figures more relevant related to your project. A good number could be around 10 figures.
Make sure your figures are clear, with axis fonts large enough. 
You can check the next tutorials about how to make nice scientific figures:
"How to Make Good Graphs and Figures for Scientific Papers": https://www.simplifiedsciencepublishing.com/resources/how-to-make-good-graphs-and-figures-for-scientific-papers
"Ten simple rules for better figures":https://doi.org/10.1136/sbmj.f297
Ask your colleagues if your figures are informative.

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx::chunk_0002
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx
text:
ific Papers": https://www.simplifiedsciencepublishing.com/resources/how-to-make-good-graphs-and-figures-for-scientific-papers
"Ten simple rules for better figures":https://doi.org/10.1136/sbmj.f297
Ask your colleagues if your figures are informative. Every figure should be really clear and people need to understand the main point in around a minute.
Try to include one big eye-catching Figure that attracts people to your poster. For example, a colorful tCycif image, or a colorful graphical abstract (like those in the cell journal's articles).
Add always figure numbers (or letters) to each one of your figures.
Each figure should have a note/legend of the result you are showing in the figure. Example: "A) Higher immune infiltration after treatment X". Add this always on the top of each figure.
5. Avoid empty spaces
Fill all possible space with your figures, do not leave any empty space in your poster.
6. Make sure to check the correct affiliations, and funding references
Include logos of the funding agencies - Check these with Anniina and all co-authors.
ADD YOUR CONTACT INFORMATION TO THE POSTER (email, X-account)
Some Important information on image quality for the poster
If an image has low DPI (e.g., 72 DPI from screenshots or other), it will look blurry when printed large (A0 poster). So increasing the DPI will give us sharper and detailed images.
How to check DPI of an image? (Mac) 
1. Open the image in Preview.
2. Click Tools in the menu bar.
3. Select Show Inspector (Command + I).
4. In the Inspector window, click on the "i" (Info) tab.
5. Look for Resolution—it will say something like 72 DPI, 300 DPI, or 600 DPI.
If the DPI is below 300, you need to increase it manually for better print quality.

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx::chunk_0003
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx
text:
Show Inspector (Command + I).
4. In the Inspector window, click on the "i" (Info) tab.
5. Look for Resolution—it will say something like 72 DPI, 300 DPI, or 600 DPI.
If the DPI is below 300, you need to increase it manually for better print quality.
Note that, only increasing DPI doesn’t improve quality—it only changes metadata. So the best fix is to increase both image size and DPI.
Step 1: Upscale Your Images Using BigJPG
Go to BigJPG.com.
Upload your image → Click ‘Start’ 
Choose ‘Photo’ mode → Set 4x upscale → Click OK.
Wait for processing → Download the upscaled image.
Despite this, if your image has not changed the DPI, you can manually do this. 
Step 2: Adjust DPI in Preview
Open the image in Preview.
Click Tools → Adjust Size.
Uncheck “Resample Image” (Important!).
Change DPI to 600.
Click OK → Save the file.
- Further reading
Here are some nice tutorials about how to make a nice poster.
"Making a better research poster": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AwMFhyH7_5g
"How to make a scientific poster": https://doi.org/10.1136/sbmj.f297

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Lab meeting presentation guidelines.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Lab meeting presentation guidelines.docx
text:
Lab meeting / presentation guidelines
Lab meetings are an integral part for your training in the lab, and the goal of these guidelines is to help improve your presentation skills. It is encouraged to ask for feedback after (actively ask or use a google form) your presentation and be prepared to receive feedback from e.g. Anniina after your presentation. 
For PhD students with several supervisors and collaborative projects: make sure you invite the other supervisors and critical collaborators to your lab meeting presentation. 
For presenting your own project, it is important to acknowledge and assume that the audience doesn’t know anything about your project, the datasets or technologies that you work with.
When presenting - please stand while you are presenting. This is practice for you to be more relaxed, natural for effective use of your voice and body while presenting. 
Background 
It is important to give background and context on your project. What is the important unmet need it addresses? 
Introduction to High-grade serous ovarian cancer can be very short
What are the main objectives of your project?
Materials and methods
Introduce all datasets as methods in sufficient detail
Include numbers of samples, patients etc and how the samples were acquired
Show principles of technologies - especially new ones - also remembering that there are always new people in the lab who have never heard of tCycIF - you can keep this short, avoid going too deep into details if they are not the main part of your presentation. A good practice is also to ask from the audience who is already familiar with this technology - and if all are - you can skip the details. 
Results

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Lab meeting presentation guidelines.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Lab meeting presentation guidelines.docx
text:
eep this short, avoid going too deep into details if they are not the main part of your presentation. A good practice is also to ask from the audience who is already familiar with this technology - and if all are - you can skip the details. 
Results
If you have several main types of results or data in your presentation, use section headers to structure your presentation. It also helps you with transitions between data/result types/sections. 
For each analysis result/plot that you show
Make sure that the plots are visible to the end row of the presentation room - use BIG graphs and fonts - typically one or two plots per slide
Use big fonts and very short sentences for text 
Include information about the type of data you are using
Include the rationale of what you investigate, and why you do the graph/analysis. What is your aim or hypothesis in that particular analysis E.g. Correlation of x and Y to reveal zzz. This can be a sentence or few words as text which you explain.
For each plot - go through the axis legends and explain in detail what is in the graph. 
For each slide - clearly state the conclusion/interpretation (what does this mean) - can also be presented as text (in the title or as a separate text/sentence).
For slides with several plots, animate the slides to introduce the data one by one, or use arrows or pointer to show the audience which part of the slide they should look at. 
For each slide - the title should be a short sentence stating the main conclusion of each slide - e.g. Immune infiltration is associated with patient outcomes. It will help the audience to see what is your main point in each slide. In results -section avoid using titles that describe only the analysis/method/material used
4) Conclusions
Summarize your main findings

chunk_id: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Lab meeting presentation guidelines.docx::chunk_0002
source_file: Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/Lab meeting presentation guidelines.docx
text:
tion is associated with patient outcomes. It will help the audience to see what is your main point in each slide. In results -section avoid using titles that describe only the analysis/method/material used
4) Conclusions
Summarize your main findings
Link your findings to the “bigger picture” - main aims and how they help to answer the unmet need 
Include future perspectives - what are you going to do or should be done next

chunk_id: Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx
text:
´Travel grants and other grant opportunities for PhD students and postdocs
Introduction
There are a variety of grants that PhD students can apply for. Travel grant calls are often advertised via email lists and organized by the doctoral schools. Personal and salary grants are funded by private foundations, and thus are advertised on their own web pages.There are grants that you can apply for salary, for example over six months up to two years. In addition there are personal encouragement grants, especially for intermediate to late-stage PhD students, intended to be a bonus on top of your salary, or to cover costs such as the Karonkka after your defence. Some encouragement grants are awarded to earlier-stage PhD students as well, and certain grants can be given to the same person more than once (see BMH foundation), so it is a good idea to begin applying for them as soon as you fit the requirements.
How to effectively apply for travel grants 
You should aim for at least one conference visit per year, and always apply for the travel grants. Please see the instructions for abstract submission. 
Often the travel grants’ listed requirements include you to have a travel itinerary, an accepted poster/talk abstract etc. However, in reality the travel grant call deadlines never line up perfectly with the conference of your choice, and basically all abstracts are accepted at least as posters. 
-> Thus it is important that you apply for all potential travel grants even prior to abstract submissions/acceptances. You need to also strategise - and apply the UH travel grant (where destination cannot be changed) to the conference which you at least plan to attend. 
If you don't have all information the travel grant asks for, you can submit estimates of travel costs.

chunk_id: Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx
text:
need to also strategise - and apply the UH travel grant (where destination cannot be changed) to the conference which you at least plan to attend. 
If you don't have all information the travel grant asks for, you can submit estimates of travel costs. The secretaries handling the costs associated with the travel grants are accustomed to adjusting the costs closer to the date of the conference, when the actual costs are known. 
Typically the travel grants should be used within 12 months (or within the calendar year if the call deadline is in Jan-Feb)
Tip#0: Write why that conference is crucial for the development of your project.
List of travel grants
Cancer Foundation of Finland - travel grant (Deadline in May)
https://syopasaatio.fi/en/homepage/for-researchers/travel-grants/
Faculty of Medicine Travel grants twice a year (UH e-form can only be awarded max once per calendar year. Deadlines typically in January-February, and late August-September) - note that you cannot change the destination/congress
Faculty of Medicine and HiLIFE travel grants for postdocs once a year (Feb-March)
The Medicine Fund Travel Support Grants (UH e-form, Deadline typically in February)
The Magnus Ehrnrooth Foundation (for long research visits)
https://magnusehrnroothinsaatio.fi/
Tips for applying to encouragement grants
You are allowed and encouraged to apply for personal grants, but as these are extra money (in addition to your salary) the applications should not take time away from your work on projects. Thus the applications should always be done outside your work hours on your own time. Anniina is happy to provide a letter of recommendation (you provide a draft), but will not have time to comment, and Joonas can do a quick round of comments for your application.

chunk_id: Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx::chunk_0002
source_file: Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx
text:
tions should always be done outside your work hours on your own time. Anniina is happy to provide a letter of recommendation (you provide a draft), but will not have time to comment, and Joonas can do a quick round of comments for your application. 
Tip #0: Write in a project-admin-tool like Trello, or and in your calendar when are the deadlines for applying to each grant. 
Tip #1: It is highly recommended that you take courses for grant writing such as those given at the University of Helsinki. In the Nature Masterclasses, which we have access till June 9th 2025 (extended longer), there is also a course about grant writing: https://masterclasses.nature.com/grant-writing/18994906
Tip#2: Prepare a super application well in advance of the deadline. Start preparing it several weeks before. Then, give it to somebody that does not know anything about your project asking for feedback. After that, improve your grant. Then store your grant for a couple of days, or weeks if possible, and then read it one last time for final improvements. Give it so much time to your application, then you can use a similar document and send it to other fundations during the year.
Tip #3: In the application, never say you are going to use the grant for karonkka costs. That will automatically ruin your chances to get any of them. After being awarded the grant, no one will know what you really use it for, so in reality you can do whatever you want with it. 
The foundations awarding the encouragement grants are typically focused on one niche topic/field or another. Read the descriptions of the grants, and format your application accordingly.

chunk_id: Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx::chunk_0003
source_file: Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx
text:
se it for, so in reality you can do whatever you want with it. 
The foundations awarding the encouragement grants are typically focused on one niche topic/field or another. Read the descriptions of the grants, and format your application accordingly. You should also be mindful that even though you might be doing your doctoral research leading to a PhD, the topic can still be clinically significant to warrant sending your application to e.g. the more medicine-focused foundations.
You might need to highlight a specific angle on your project for some of the applications, whereas a different aspect might be more suitable for another grant. E.g. highlighting the potential avenues for targeted therapies, or the basic biology/immunology. If the foundation asks for justification as to why you should be awarded the grant, assess what the foundation wants to support, and try to mirror that in your justification.
Tip #4: Prepare a grant with only the scopes of your first project and some perspectives. The next year you could also apply with a new project or collaboration you are performing. Usually foundations do not give grants twice to the same project
List of encouragement grants
You could maybe find more foundations in Research.fi (https://research.fi/en/)
General:
The Maud Kuistila Memorial Foundation (deadline in January)
https://mkmsaatio.fi/en/research-grants/
Idan Montinin Säätiö (deadline at the start of March)
http://www.idamontininsaatio.fi/pages/suomi/etusivu.php 
Biomedicum Helsinki Foundation (deadline in the Spring, like in April)
https://biomedicum.com/participate/biomedicum-helsinki-foundation-grants/ 
Cancer Foundation of Finland (Deadline in the end of August)
https://syopasaatio.fi/en/homepage/
Instrumentarium (deadline early September)

chunk_id: Research-related/Guidelines how to host a Third Reviewer Club or TRC.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Guidelines how to host a Third Reviewer Club or TRC.docx
text:
Guidelines how to host a Third Reviewer Club or TRC
As the TRC host, your role is to facilitate and lead the discussion on the selected publication. 
The TRC slots are divided as follows starting from spring term 2026, and slots ideally distributed latest during the last general meeting of the prior season
TRC slot themes:
Clinical
Bioinformatics
Wet lab/functional
Responsibilities of the person hosting is to
Choose the paper to be discussed within the theme of the slot 
Focus on the specific parts that are relevant to you - the host chooses the focus and learning objectives of the TRC
Ensure that the audience can complete their read-through of the paper and your questions in a maximum of two hours - plan the scope and questions accordingly
Create e.g. a Google Sheets file to the TRC folder (Third reviewer clubs) that contains: 
One-slide summary of the paper
Learning objectives for the TRC
A maximum of 5 questions, including the question “Which of the methods/approaches in this article do you think might be worth implementing in our own research?” i.e. come up with a maximum 4 questions from the article
Share the paper along with the completed TRC presentation one week before the TRC in Slack, in the #third-reviewer-journal-club channel
Chair the TRC in a way that ensures that everyone has to say what they thought about the paper and answer at least one question.
If you have any questions about choosing the scope of the paper/discussion, or the questions, please contact Joonas
Note that for hosting TRC:s you can get study credits towards your PhD! Mark down the hours for preparation and hosting!

chunk_id: Research-related/Meeting guidelines/How to set up and prepare for PhD Thesis committee meeting.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Meeting guidelines/How to set up and prepare for PhD Thesis committee meeting.docx
text:
Booking the meeting
Book 1 month in advance 
Check our internal calendars 
Lab common and Anniina’s own for tentative slots
Anniina’s typical working hours are from 9-17 - if needed you can ask for exceptions
No more than 6 meetings per day - if urgent you can ask for exceptions
Confirm the slots with Anniina using Slack private message.
Generate the doodle
No more than 1x1h tentative slots per day
Spread the the slots over min 3 days and 2 weeks - this allows more flexibility for busy PIs 
After the doodle is finalised, remove the tentative slots
After setting the date -send an Outlook calendar invitation (with zoom/teams link) to the TC members
Preparation for the meeting
Fill your thessa report https://thessa.helsinki.fi/ "my PhD project" 1 week before the thesis committee meeting.
Prepare a presentation with the following updates since the previous year
1-2 slides per topic, presentation max 20 minutes. 
Preliminary title of the PhD
Brief background and overall aims 
Publications plan (published, submitted, in preparation, planned)
Conferences (national, international, posters, talks)
Funding (salary, grants)
Completed and planned PhD courses
Other courses and workshops
Risks and mitigation strategies 
Timetable of the PhD - GANTT chart
In the end of TC - there should be 15mins reserved for three different discussions
5 mins - without PhD candidate (supervisors + TC)
5-8 mins - without supervisors (PhD Candidate + TC)
2-5 min - final conclusion
Note that typically scientific results are not presented. However, it is encouraged to ask for the committee members' expert opinion on one project / scientific/methods question.

chunk_id: Research-related/Meeting guidelines/How to set up and prepare for PhD Thesis committee meeting.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Meeting guidelines/How to set up and prepare for PhD Thesis committee meeting.docx
text:
s - without supervisors (PhD Candidate + TC)
2-5 min - final conclusion
Note that typically scientific results are not presented. However, it is encouraged to ask for the committee members' expert opinion on one project / scientific/methods question. Ideally, this question will help you in experimental design, methodological approaches, or interpretation of the results, depending on the expertise of the committee members and research topic.
A conclusion of the report should be written at the end/after the thesis committee meeting to https://thessa.helsinki.fi/ (a few paragraphs). The conclusion should include the comments made by the thesis committee members.

chunk_id: Research-related/Meeting guidelines/Meeting SOP.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Meeting guidelines/Meeting SOP.docx
text:
SOP for meetings 
General guidelines
For thesis committees and meetings with external collaborations, Remember to send Outlook calendar invitations (with zoom/teams link).
Every project (and thus the project meetings too) needs a clear project lead. The project lead chairs meetings and prepares and shares the agenda for every meeting in advance. It is also the duty of every attendee to read the agenda in advance to use the meeting time effectively.
The agenda should include clear information on the aim and contents of the meeting, so everyone can understand the progression of the meeting
The agenda can be posted to the slack channel of the project, or the calendar booking in Google Calendar, as long as everyone attending the meeting knows where to find the agenda
Mark in Google calendar what the topic is and who is joining. Please use complete words/sentences that describe the goal of the meeting - this makes it possible for everyone to prepare For example - NNN project meeting to check results of XXX and decide YYYY- Anniina, Ada, Joonas 
When chairing a meeting:
Lead the discussion through the agenda prepared and shared in advance - make sure you track the time
First 5-10 minutes are used to summarise the big picture, overall goals of the meeting and to present the agenda
Last 5-10 minutes are used to
recap the conclusions
make sure everyone is aligned with the action items and understand their own tasks
book the next meeting if needed
Booking a meeting with Anniina 
Book in advance 
It is easiest to find free slots within 1-2 weeks
It is encouraged to book the next meeting in the end of the previous meeting
Check calendars 
Lab common and Anniina’s own for tentative slots
Anniina’s typical working hours are from 9-17 - if needed you can ask for exceptions

It is encouraged to book the next meeting in the end of the previous meeting

No more than 6 meetings per day - if urgent you can ask for exceptions
The max duration of effective meetings is 55min (1h slot) - ask personally for exceptions. Confirm the slot with Anniina using Slack private message.
