chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx
text:
Congratulations you have reached the point of writing your thesis on your PhD journey!
PhD Thesis considerations
The thesis is a synthesis of your PhD work, not just a plain summary. The goal is to merge your projects/publications into one synthesis.
The thesis should clearly present your scientific contributions, and your maturity as a scientist. Here is a link to some of the criteria that the pre-examiners will use to evaluate the doctoral thesis: https://www.utu.fi/sites/default/files/media/med_pre-examiner_instructions.pdf
The thesis is your own production, and I will generally give you only “big picture feedback” to make sure it will pass the faculty requirements and pre-examination. 
In general, you should use the minimum required time for writing the thesis. Except for your supervisor(s), the thesis examiners and your opponent, it is likely that no-one else will ever read your whole thesis. 🙂
In general, for the first submission to faculty, the thesis needs to “look like a thesis”, and you can keep editing it during the pre-examination.
How should I get started?
Well planned is half done!
Firstly, check the desired time of defence and then count back the timelines according to the faculty instructions. Your most important deadline is when to leave the thesis for pre-examination. 
Start by making a table of contents, and think carefully about what to focus on - these are the main findings/conclusions of your PhD, and things you will be ideally discussing at the defence. The suggested page number is between 40-60 - from beginning of introduction, including figures and excluding references. 
The overall structure should follow the faculty template. Here are some insights: 
For Objectives: enlist at least one Aim for each publication

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx
text:
ge number is between 40-60 - from beginning of introduction, including figures and excluding references. 
The overall structure should follow the faculty template. Here are some insights: 
For Objectives: enlist at least one Aim for each publication
For Background/Introduction (15-20 pages): Plan and enlist the topics you need to cover focusing on the main scientific findings of your publications. Write out how many pages you plan to write for each section of the background so that it will be balanced. Include 1, max 2 illustrations of the main concepts. 
For Materials and Methods (5-10 pages), aim to combine the methods from the publications. Tables are ideal for this purpose. Note that you need to briefly explain methods which you did not perform (bioinformatics/wetlab) if they are parts of the publication. 
For the Results and Discussion (20-30 pages): I suggest combining results and discussion. Plan to include 2-3 main findings from each publication. You can copy-paste images from the publications, but select subpanels, do not use whole figures. Then add one chapter for describing the results and one chapter for discussing the relevance of the result in the context of current knowledge. Feel free to copy-paste text from original publications, but then edit the text to not be the same. 
How much time should I allocate for writing? 
Overall, the thesis should be written in a time equaling 1 month full time work. 
After making the table of contents, you should make a writing plan - plan to write e.g. 2-3 pages every day or distribute this across the week together with your project work, so that you write e.g. 1-2 days a week.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx::chunk_0002
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx
text:
qualing 1 month full time work. 
After making the table of contents, you should make a writing plan - plan to write e.g. 2-3 pages every day or distribute this across the week together with your project work, so that you write e.g. 1-2 days a week. 
Make clear deadlines for yourself, and inform me when you will be sending each section for my review, and keep me updated about potential delays. 
I strongly encourage you to start with the Results and Discussion - this will make it easier for you to focus the introduction on the same topics. Then put together the Methods and Introduction accordingly. 
Planning together and getting feedback from me?
First, we will go through the table of contents together, and plan the defence date, pre-examiners and opponent. 
I will email the opponent and pre-examiners - for this I need your tentative title.
Please share your writing plan and timeline for sending each section to me. 
For feedback on the thesis, I prefer to review each full section at a time. 
Finally, I will read once more the whole thesis before you send it to the faculty. 
At any point- if you get stuck, don't know what to focus on, don't hesitate to ask me!
A common struggle is that when starting to write the introduction, you get overwhelmed and think that you should cover everything. That is where the structure and plan should help you to focus. Me and the pre-examiners will then also give feedback if we feel that something is missing. 
Enlisting your contributions to each publication:
It is important to clearly enlist all your contributions to each publication. You can follow the below list for contributions: 

Below is an outline of the main components in scientific publishing, which can help in assigning and acknowledging contributions accurately.
1.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx::chunk_0003
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx
text:
arly enlist all your contributions to each publication. You can follow the below list for contributions: 

Below is an outline of the main components in scientific publishing, which can help in assigning and acknowledging contributions accurately.
1. Conceptualization & Study Design
Formulating the research question and hypothesis
Designing the study methodology and objectives
Establishing the experimental or analytical approach
2. Data Acquisition & Investigation
Conducting experiments, collecting samples, or gathering data
Performing computational analyses, simulations, or fieldwork
Running laboratory assays, imaging, sequencing, or data extraction
3. Data Analysis & Interpretation
Processing and analyzing raw data
Performing statistical modeling, bioinformatics, or AI-driven analysis
Interpreting results and drawing conclusions
4. Software & Code Development (if applicable)
Writing or modifying software tools, scripts, or pipelines
Developing models, algorithms, or databases used in the study
5. Writing – Original Draft
Preparing the initial manuscript draft
Writing the introduction, methods, results, and discussion
6. Writing – Review & Editing
Revising manuscript content for clarity, coherence, and scientific accuracy
Editing figures, tables, and references
Addressing reviewer comments and journal revisions
7. Visualization & Figures
Designing figures, graphs, illustrations, and schematics
Preparing data visualizations and supplementary materials
8. Supervision & Project Administration
Overseeing research execution and ensuring quality control
Managing the research team and facilitating collaboration
9. Funding Acquisition
Securing grants and financial support for the study
Managing research budgets and institutional approvals
10.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx::chunk_0004
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/Guidelines for PhD Thesis writing by Anniina.docx
text:
Overseeing research execution and ensuring quality control
Managing the research team and facilitating collaboration
9. Funding Acquisition
Securing grants and financial support for the study
Managing research budgets and institutional approvals
10. Ethical Approvals & Regulatory Compliance (if applicable)
Securing IRB (Institutional Review Board) or ethical committee approvals
Ensuring compliance with data protection, patient confidentiality, and ethical guidelines
11. Resources & Material Provision
Providing critical reagents, datasets, tools, or equipment
Accessing and curating publicly available datasets
12. Correspondence & Submission
Preparing and submitting the manuscript to the journal
Managing communications with the journal, handling peer review process

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx
text:
1. Choose your topic wisely and plan in advance
Browse for the possible dates online and ask your programme's coordinator about the last date to graduate on time in the current academic year (for TRANSMEDs in 2023 this was May)
Have a meeting with Anniina as soon as you end your initial training/internship/advanced research methods. Try to look together for a detailed research question or objective that you can accomplish in the agreed time frame, typically 4-6 months. 
The best Master's thesis tend to be focusing in a very narrow problem that they can frame nicely in the introduction and study with various methods
Keep in mind that your thesis can also be methodological (developing a pipeline, optimizing tCycIF experiments...) and it is also a valid approach, even though the writing is a bit different.
You can check in e-Thesis for other thesis similar to your project to get an idea of which is the expected extent 
Write your thesis plan accordingly
In TRANSMED studies, this is not really evaluated. Plans vary a lot from what you design in the beginning as well. Keep it as broad as possible to avoid future problems and do not waste a lot of time on writing this.
If the experiments have a wet-lab component plan at least 2 months to order the reagents and select and order the samples. In this time I suggest, if you are a TRANSMED student, that you work on your Research Prpposal Examination to save time (yes, this does not necessarily need to be the last thing you do).
Add one more month to your timeline just to review the content and get feedback
In general, for TRANSMED students, I suggest scheduling the presentation of the thesis plan and results for the time when you are done with the project and you are writing.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx
text:
d one more month to your timeline just to review the content and get feedback
In general, for TRANSMED students, I suggest scheduling the presentation of the thesis plan and results for the time when you are done with the project and you are writing. This way you can just summarize your introduction in the thesis plan presentation, and then summarize the results section in the thesis result session. It will also help you modify some things, reexplain or add more information according to the public's feedback.
2. Document EVERYTHING
Seems quite evident, but being organized and keeping track of how you did your experiments / analysis will help you a lot. Document all of your steps (antibody validations, experiments, analysis ...) even though you think that something should not be in your thesis initially, it might. And after two weeks you will probably not remember the parameter you used for an analysis and you will have to waste your time trying to find it. Name your files correctly, and keep a backup of everything you do. 
3. Think of the structure before you write
I used to try to write everything perfectly from the beginning, with a very vague structure of contents in my mind. Fortunately, I got very good tips at the beginning of the thesis writing process from both @casado@helsinki_fi and @nagaraj@helsinki_fi that were incredibly useful. These are the ones that helped me the most:
Before writing you need to draft a clear structure with the points that you need to cover in each of the parts of the thesis. For example, I was dividing my introduction in A) ovarian cancer background B) TME and C) Spatially-resolved methodologies. However, there are subtopics in each of them.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx::chunk_0002
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx
text:
cture with the points that you need to cover in each of the parts of the thesis. For example, I was dividing my introduction in A) ovarian cancer background B) TME and C) Spatially-resolved methodologies. However, there are subtopics in each of them. In ovarian cancer, I had to introduce some epidemiologic data, information about the subtypes, tumor's genetics, standard treatments... in B) TME, I had to explain what it was, its components, the relevance of the immune compartment and the main cells that I was going to study (and for each of them its subtypes...). Writing this draft made it easier to get feedback and , even before writing. I had too many irrelevant points in mind (like diagnostic procedures, which were not very related to my project, or deep information about cell types that I was not exploring), and it was great to get these reduced before beginning to write. It also helped me to browse articles in a more specific way to find interesting discoveries related to my research question.
After you get the draft of the structure reviewed, you need to write one paragraph for each point with the information that you are interested in. There are two points that I found great to speed up the process.
First, I wrote without worrying about the grammar, vocabulary, connectors etc, and I just explain the content.
Second, I wrote an introductory sentence that anticipates what I was going to explain. I added a final sentence in the end of the paragraph, either summarizing or linking the explained ideas with the broad topic again. Later on time, I came back to the English and improved my writing style. Once the contents and the order were clear this process was smoother.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx::chunk_0003
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx
text:
n the end of the paragraph, either summarizing or linking the explained ideas with the broad topic again. Later on time, I came back to the English and improved my writing style. Once the contents and the order were clear this process was smoother. 
Additional tips: consider using ChatGPT or Grammarly to correct your English or rephrase some of your paragraphs, it is fast and it will significantly improve the final result
Some interesting feedback that I got after my first draft was focused on my results section. For example, one of my headings was "Antibody validation", this ended up as "tCycIF antibody validation and image processing allows the coherent characterization of the target cell-types".
4. Remember that you don't need the results to be ready in order to write the introduction
I wrote most of my introduction/literature review in November, while my thesis was handed in May! And I believe this was a great choice. In the introduction/literature review (TRANSMED MSc) you comment on the state of the art in the field you are doing your thesis in, and explain why your work is needed. Writing the review part in the initial stages of the project will save you time later on. Also, reading and writing will give you a nice background and will help you throughout the project. Even if your experimental plans change, some topics , like HGSC or TME should be reviewed, so it will be useful material no matter what. 
In April, when the rest of the sections were complete, I read again my literature review and wrote an introduction in a similar style to that in any scientific paper. Additionally, I added a parragraph titled "significance and challenges" that had a good feedback from both my supervisors and the reviewers.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx::chunk_0004
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx
text:
read again my literature review and wrote an introduction in a similar style to that in any scientific paper. Additionally, I added a parragraph titled "significance and challenges" that had a good feedback from both my supervisors and the reviewers. Therefore, I suggest paying attention to this part and adapting it to your own thesis.
5. Define your aims clearly and concretely 
Thesis Aim and Objectives are really critical - the aims should be clarified early on while planning the thesis, and in the end they should be answered in the results. 
First a short paragraph defining the Overall Aim: State the broad goal of the research. What is the overarching aim of your thesis?
Example: "The main aim of this thesis is to explore the molecular mechanisms of immune evasion in HGSC."
Break Down the Main Aim into Specific Objectives: List the key specific aims that contribute to achieving your main research objective. These should outline the steps or components of your research.
Example:
"To identify the immune cell populations present in HGSC tumor samples using spatial transcriptomics."
"To analyze the interaction between tumor cells and immune cells in the tumor microenvironment (TME)."
"To investigate how chemotherapy influences the immune responses in HGSC patients."
6. Design the figures
As well as the draft for the content I would suggest to think of the figures that you will add in your thesis for the Introduction, Methods or Results sections. Some tools I found useful were:
Biorender for diagrams and figures that require the use of bio-related icons

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx::chunk_0005
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx
text:
for the content I would suggest to think of the figures that you will add in your thesis for the Introduction, Methods or Results sections. Some tools I found useful were:
Biorender for diagrams and figures that require the use of bio-related icons
InkScape for the figure panels in the results section (plots etc): I was shocked when I learned that, if you export your figures in PDF and then put them in Inkscape you can change the labels, colors etc with no need to re-run your code again!
PowerPoint is also useful for any kind of figures, but I used it especially in the results section
If you need to take many image crops for a t-CycIF related thesis, consider utilizing QuPath paired with ImageJ (See sending regions to ImageJ ) and saving the files afterwards. 
Always keep in mind these things when creating the figures:
The legends and labeling are clear, and the message can be easily understood
The text and the image are big enough to appreciate, and the quality is good
The image caption is explanatory
Most likely you will need to make various versions, so make code where you can easily replace the values, and save the images independently, together with the contrast limits when you work with images
Here is one possible way to export figures with relatively high-resolution from PPT to your thesis in Word/PDF.
Select all elements on one page in the PowerPoint.
Combine them as a group so there is only one big figure for one page.
Right-click this big figure and select Save as Image (S).
In the pop-up save window, change the saved image type from the default .png to enhanced metafile format (*.emf).

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx::chunk_0006
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx
text:
rPoint.
Combine them as a group so there is only one big figure for one page.
Right-click this big figure and select Save as Image (S).
In the pop-up save window, change the saved image type from the default .png to enhanced metafile format (*.emf).
Insert this .emf figure to your thesis in Word/PDF, and you will see this figure has better resolution compared to png or jpeg, especially if there are some big t-cycif images in this figure.
6. Use a reference manager 
Such as Zotero, Mendeley, PaperPal since the very beginning. I like Zotero a lot, so I used it while reading to save the articles in my library with the Google Chrome extension, and then I added the citations while writing (in google docs/word)
7. Choose your reviewers
Once you are near submission, but with enough time in advance, you should begin contacting the reviewers (at least this is the procedure in the TRANSMED programme). Check your programme's rules and chat with Anniina so that she suggests adequate reviewers for your work.
8. Which feedback did reviewers provide? You can now avoid these mistakes
I have also attached my grading and comments, so that you can see which things they found positive and which they found were lacking. Some of them are more related to their personal taste. I would recommend these to future MSc thesis students based on my result:
Put all your energy in making a very good scientific framing of the problem, highlighting the significance of the thesis and outlining why this research or new method is needed
In TRANSMED, people that initiate their thesis with a brief literature review (15-20pp) usually do better than those with a brief introduction.
Think of your figures wisely (I was told for example, I could have added some more figures in the introduction)

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx::chunk_0007
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/How to master your Masters thesis.docx
text:
NSMED, people that initiate their thesis with a brief literature review (15-20pp) usually do better than those with a brief introduction.
Think of your figures wisely (I was told for example, I could have added some more figures in the introduction)
They expect very detailed methods section. Add every detail, even if you think it is irrelevant. For example, I said I used Ashlar for stitching, but I did not explain the maximum shift value or the other arguments I was introducing. This kind of information, even though it is not included in the methods section of a paper, should be introduced in the thesis.
The most important: In my case the results section was the Achilles tendon of the thesis, the reviewers wanted more analysis of the results I obtained with my test data in my new tool. However, I was solely presenting that the tool had worked. This was their specific claim "The thesis was quite descriptive in nature. No clear analysis or comparison aspect was brought up". Analyzing in some way the output images (they mentioned some options) could have been a good addition to my thesis. I recommend very much considering this in methodological thesis.
@junqmada@helsinki_fi TRANSMED Master's thesis: 
JunqueraMencia_Ada_thesis_2023.pdf
evaluation.pdf

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx
text:
PhD Defense protocol in short form for the PhD candidate
Ensure that that all the systems (mic, stream, etc.) are functional as much in advance of the defense as possible. In the Haartman institute, the stewards are very helpful, at Biomedicum less so.
Note: Bolded underlined are lines that the PhD candidate says during the public examination, learn by heart, for the situation where your heart desires to exist outside your chest..
Faculty guidelines: https://www.helsinki.fi/en/research/doctoral-school/doctoral-studies-university-helsinki/welcome-public-examination
Entrance
At the actual start of the defense, a quarter past the hour (to ensure the entire attendance is seated before the show begins):
Candidate walks in first, custos second, opponent last. Custos and opponent hold the doctor’s hats in their hands, all three place the dissertation binders to the assigned table positions, along with the hats. Candidate takes the far side place from the door, custos sits middle next to the meeting controls, opponent at the near position to the door.
Custos: Introduces him/herself, the defendant and the opponent "As the Custos appointed by the Faculty of Medicine, I declare this public examination open."
Custos and opponent sit down before the candidate begins the lectio
Opening words of the lectio - the candidate says the following words: "Honored Custos, honored Opponent, members of the audience" 
Lectio praecursoria
After the lectio, the candidate says the following words: "Honored Opponent, I now call upon you to present your critical comments on my dissertation."
Opponents opening statement - candidate stays standing for the statement, then both the opponent and the candidate sit down for the start of the examination
Public examination i.e. The Grilling

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx
text:
to present your critical comments on my dissertation."
Opponents opening statement - candidate stays standing for the statement, then both the opponent and the candidate sit down for the start of the examination
Public examination i.e. The Grilling
The opponent dictates the whole thing, the candidate abides.
Opponent’s closing statement
After the public examination, the opponent should ask the candidate to stand up, and stand up himself as well. The opponent will then make a final statement and will announce that he or she will propose to the Faculty that the dissertation be accepted. 
After the candidate thanks the opponent for the examination, the audience to make comments and pose questions: "If anyone present wishes to make any comments concerning my dissertation, please ask the Custos for the floor."
After the (potential) questions:
Closing
The custos will stand up to announce that the examination is completed. The candidate can inform the audience of sparkling wine + cake being served, if such are organized to follow the defense.
The custos and the opponent will carry their Doctor's hats and dissertation binders when leaving the auditorium in the same order in which they entered: the doctoral candidate will leave first, followed by the custos and the opponent. The candidate, custos, and opponent should go directly to the spot where the sparkling + cake is to be had to avoid a logistical nightmare in which 20 people are trying to congratulate the candidate while the candidate attempts to move the mass of people to the site of the cake.
The audience must not applaud or cheer during the public examination. (Do tell this to your friends/family in audience not familiar with the concept or don’t…)
Karonkka

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx::chunk_0002
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx
text:
while the candidate attempts to move the mass of people to the site of the cake.
The audience must not applaud or cheer during the public examination. (Do tell this to your friends/family in audience not familiar with the concept or don’t…)
Karonkka
Even though this is your (read: the PhD candidate’s) hour, officially the karonkka is held in the honor of the opponent. 
The program of the formal dinner, and the seating arrangement is up to you, however certain traditions are still normally honored. 
The opponent traditionally sits at the seat of honor on the right of the candidate. Custos is seated either across, or to the left of the candidate depending on the venue and tables available. After that, you can pick your order, typically the people with the most academic achievements sit the closest to the candidate (however, place your supervisor(s) as close to you as possible if you do not hate them), then move down the “merit list” from there. 
In reality when progressing further down the merit list, you should consider how to make people enjoy their time at the dinner and seat people accordingly. Family and friends are typically further away, as this is an academic event in the end. Remember to make sure your spouse/family/friends do not feel isolated - remember that they are totally lost on everything happening during this day if they are not working in the field - try to place familiar people/labrats with social skills close to them. Do remember to let your significant other know of this quirk of the seating in advance to avoid a fun night.
The candidate welcomes everyone at the start, then the starters are served, followed by the main course. 
It is customary for the candidate to give a small gift to the opponent, which is usually something very Finnish (e.g.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx::chunk_0003
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx
text:
ance to avoid a fun night.
The candidate welcomes everyone at the start, then the starters are served, followed by the main course. 
It is customary for the candidate to give a small gift to the opponent, which is usually something very Finnish (e.g. a kuksa or something of the sort).
Speeches
After the main is typically the time for the speeches. It would be wise to let less experienced people discreetly know to go to the restroom before the mains are done and the speeches begin, as the speeches can take a while. If you have a venue with servers around, make sure they know of the agenda, and that they should offer to fill the cups before the speeches, as traditionally after every speech, there is a toast. This means that after thanking the opponent, there is a toast, then another toast after thanking the custos and so on. The candidate can also have all the speeches in a row without toasts, but the toasts form the rhythm especially for the responses and let people know what is going on. For the candidate, raising a toast after each part of the speech also gives a tiny moment of respite during what can easily be ~20 minutes of non-stop talking.
First, the candidate does his/her speeches, in the order of 1) opponent 2) custos 3) supervisor(s) 4) anything goes - typically in the approximate order of seating/importance. Thanks to family and friends are typically left for last.
After the candidate has finished their round of speeches, the responses commence, in the order of the candidates speeches. After the last speech is done, the dessert is served.
A tradition during the speeches is that if the candidate names a person during their speech, this named person traditionally answers with a speech of their own on their turn.

chunk_id: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx::chunk_0004
source_file: Research-related/Theses guidelines/PhD Defense + karonkka protocol.docx
text:
candidates speeches. After the last speech is done, the dessert is served.
A tradition during the speeches is that if the candidate names a person during their speech, this named person traditionally answers with a speech of their own on their turn. However the candidate is encouraged to let people know in advance how the speeches are going to play out, i.e. who are the people expected to speak. Especially in the case of referring to the lab of the candidate, if no names are dropped during the speech, letting the people from the lab in attendance know of this allows them to prepare their own response speeches as a group, i.e. which members of the lab want to speak and so forth.
NOTE: do let less experienced people know that they can expect approx. 12 - 20 toasts, so slamming down half a glass of wine per toast is probably a bad idea - or a good one, i leave that to everyone’s own judgement
Program
Typically, the lab of the candidate organizes some kind of a number if the candidate wants there to be something. Is usually a lighthearted trip down the memory lane. Can be during the dessert, or at the start of the afterparty, depending on the venue etc.

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/Holidays and APM.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Work-related guidelines/Holidays and APM.docx
text:
Taking time off is important for family, rest, maintaining resilience and motivation. 
Please see below some general information and instructions: 
What is the amount of holidays?
As a general rule the amount of paid holidays for university research personnel is approximately 5-7 weeks per year (Sept-Sept), depending on your contract.
This can be distributed across the year as you see best and agree with your supervisor(s).
For part-time students, the paid holiday days are proportional to your working %
For short-term internships, you can calculate that 2 days of paid holiday are accumulated per month and use these before the end of your internship.
Marking the holidays to SAP (Only for UH salaried personnel)
Expert and support personnel
Check your leave entitlement in SAP Fiori. The final holiday entitlement for the holiday year 1.4-31.3 can be seen after 31.3.
Propose the dates of your leave to your supervisor.
Once your supervisor has accepted your leave plan, submit an application for annual leave in SAP Fiori https://msap.sap.helsinki.fi/.
Research and teaching personnel
Change the hour allocation in SAP Fiori https://msap.sap.helsinki.fi/:
Employee >> Working hours, work plan, multi-location work and telecommuting >> Working time allocation >> Click on each vacation day, open the “Type” dropdown menu, and select “Holiday Equivalent”, and select “Confirm allocations” from the bottom right of the screen.
Flamma instructions on annual leave: https://flamma.helsinki.fi/en/group/henkilostoasiat/annual-leave
How to plan and go on holidays?
Agree your holiday days with your supervisor(s) and with everyone working on the same collaborative projects as early as possible.
Prioritise taking your holidays during summer (July) and other general holiday periods if possible.

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/Holidays and APM.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Work-related guidelines/Holidays and APM.docx
text:
d go on holidays?
Agree your holiday days with your supervisor(s) and with everyone working on the same collaborative projects as early as possible.
Prioritise taking your holidays during summer (July) and other general holiday periods if possible. 
Mark your holidays into the lab google calendar as whole day events with your name and - Holidays. 
Think of your projects/responsibilities - do you need a substitute, and who you will assign the project/data/responsibilities during your leave. 
Generate and activate an out-of-office message with an alternative point of contact (your substitute/lab phone etc) - it is critical that ppl outside of the lab know that you are absent. 
During your Holiday disconnect from work email & Slack via notification settings. 
Enjoy!

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/How to mark your sick leave.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Work-related guidelines/How to mark your sick leave.docx
text:
General considerations for sick days
If you are sick, it is best to stay at home and recover, especially if you are down with a virus. General rule is that if you are not able to work at >80% efficiency, it is better to take a sick day. 
It is important to send a message to Anniina if you are on sick leave - regardless of your funding/grant/salary. This is to ensure resource allocation and smooth running of the projects.
Marking of sick leave: Short leaves of 5 days or less: (only applicable to those on UH salary)
Change the hour allocation in SAP Fiori https://msap.sap.helsinki.fi/:
Employee >> Working hours, work plan, multi-location work and telecommuting >> Working time allocation >> Click on each sick day, open the “Type” dropdown menu, and select “Sick leave”, and select “Confirm allocations” from the bottom right of the screen.
Absence notification in SAP Fiori https://msap.sap.helsinki.fi/:
Employee >> Leaves and absences >> My Leave Requests >> Create Request (bottom right) >> Fill the form, select “Illness” in the top dropdown menu
Other instructions at https://flamma.helsinki.fi/en/group/henkilostoasiat/absence-due-to-illness

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/How to use the lab credit card.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Work-related guidelines/How to use the lab credit card.docx
text:
Usage guidelines for the lab credit card
What
The lab’s credit card is a personal card given to Anniina by UH that can be used to pay for goods and services without an invoice. The card is to be used for non-invoiceable work-related costs only (e.g. conference registrations, conference accommodation booking).
The card functions as a standard credit card, but allows for the direction of the costs to UH-administered grants after the invoice arrives. Saun handles the invoices from the credit card, please always send your receipts of credit card purchases to Saun for processing.
Whenever using the card, follow these guidelines to avoid locking the card, since the unlocking process is laborious, and only Anniina can unlock the card. If you run into issues at any point during the use of the lab card, contact Joonas right away instead of trying to e.g. repeat payment steps.
Note: Several safety triggers WILL lock the card: 
Several (near-)simultaneous payment requests from different IP addresses - only one person uses the card to pay at once, never spam payment requests if you get errors during the process, always contact Joonas if there are any issues.
Foreign IP addresses will block the use of the card - only use the card when you are physically in Finland (VPN won’t work). 
Failed (2-factor timeouted or rejected) payment requests will lock the card - coordinate payment requests with Joonas in advance. 
How
Get approval for credit card use from Anniina. 
To get Anniina’s approval, send Anniina a message in Slack containing the justification of the credit card usage, sum of the cost, and source of funding. 
Get the card details from Joonas after Anniina’s approval and agree for a 2-factor authentication time slot with Joonas

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/How to use the lab credit card.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Work-related guidelines/How to use the lab credit card.docx
text:
send Anniina a message in Slack containing the justification of the credit card usage, sum of the cost, and source of funding. 
Get the card details from Joonas after Anniina’s approval and agree for a 2-factor authentication time slot with Joonas 
Joonas needs to be aware and available of the incoming payment request for the 2FA approval
The request has a 5-minute timeout, so make sure Joonas knows the time the request will go out
If there are several people making requests at once (e.g. to register to the same conference), take coordinated payment turns to avoid simultaneous payment requests locking the card

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/Tips for international members.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Work-related guidelines/Tips for international members.docx
text:
This page aims to give new lab members suggestions on how to settle down in Helsinki. - especially when you are moving from abroad. 
Paperwork
In Finland you will need e-Identification for everything: from getting your electronic public transport tickets, to hiring a home insurance or booking a doctor's appointment. 
The way to get this identification is to get a Kela number and open a Finnish bank account. 
EU resident
Before arrive
Book your appointments at DVV and Migri to register your permanent address and get your Kela number (ASAP)
Tip: when you fill the application form you can write your current address abroad if you do not have one in Finland yet
Book your bank appointment already, try to get a date a few days after your Migri appointment, do not wait to get your kela number to do so
When you arrive
Go to your appointments and patiently wait
Go to the bank and get the account, they will give you the instructions on the e-Identification directly at the bank's office
Non-EU resident
Before arrive
Apply for the residence permit
Find housing
If you have the student identity: HOAS
If you are not a student: 
Sign the working contract (if possible)
Book an appointment at DVV
When you arrive
Go to INTERNATIONAL HOUSE HELSINKI
DVV is on the 2nd floor: register your permanent address (takes 2 weeks for workers)
You can apply for your Kela card at the same time. 
Leave INTERNATIONAL HOUSE HELSINKI, walk around 3 mins to Helsinki Area Tax Office
Get your tax card
**This is very important otherwise your salary will be taxed at 60%. 
Once DVV noticed your address was registered
You can buy the monthly ticket on the HSL card
You can visit Helsinki Police Department to apply for an ID card. 
Remember to have a Finnish phone number when you apply for the ID card.

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/Tips for international members.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: Work-related guidelines/Tips for international members.docx
text:
l be taxed at 60%. 
Once DVV noticed your address was registered
You can buy the monthly ticket on the HSL card
You can visit Helsinki Police Department to apply for an ID card. 
Remember to have a Finnish phone number when you apply for the ID card. They do not provide email notifications. 
Once your ID card is ready
Apply for your bank account and your e-identification. 
Banks
OP-Pankki has free services until 26 y.o. , as well as a free opening fee, and it is the recommended bank for students. To open an account you must wait for some weeks, so make sure you book an appointment beforehand.
S-Pankki has free lifetime services, but you need to pay ~100 euros to get your account. However, they will return this money to you after closing the account. No need to book an appointment for this bank
No English services are provided 
@junqmada@helsinki_fi 's experience in 2021 was good at S-Pankki sokos. She went to the bank with no appointment and received the online keys within one week of the appointment
Nordea
Housing
Housing options:
Home insurance recommendations: Pohjola, IF, 
Well-connected areas to our campus in Helsinki: Meilahti, Taka-Töölö, Ruskeasuo, Pikku Huopalahti, Pasila, Alppila, Pitäjänmäki, Konala, Kannelmäki
Well-connected areas to our campus in Vantaa: Kaivoksela
Well-connected areas to our campus in Espoo: 
Climate conditions and clothing
Except for the summer months, the weather in Finland is quite cold, and Helsinki being next to the Baltic sea means that wind/rainproof clothes are a factor that increases quality of life by a lot, also proper padded winter clothes should be acquired for the winter months, even day temperatures can drop to ~-25 °C in the winter months for extended periods of time.
Healthcare in Finland
Phone companies

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/Tips for international members.docx::chunk_0002
source_file: Work-related guidelines/Tips for international members.docx
text:
r that increases quality of life by a lot, also proper padded winter clothes should be acquired for the winter months, even day temperatures can drop to ~-25 °C in the winter months for extended periods of time.
Healthcare in Finland
Phone companies
You will probably need a pre-paid card before you can hire the services (since e-Identification is needed to establish a contract). Pre-paid services usually range between 25-30 euros/month and include unlimited data and, sometimes, unlimited calls.
They can be bought in any of the company stores or sometimes even at other stores such as TOKMANNI.
@junqmada@helsinki_fi has tried DNA pre-paid services in the past and has had a good experience.
Once you get your phone number and Bank account you can create a MobilePay account, which is a widely-used online payment platform
Public transport options to Meilahti campus
HSL route planner (available as a mobile app too) can be used to navigate the public transport system in addition to google maps - HSL.fi 
Meals: supermarkets & student-priced restaurants
On the campus, there are student priced restaurants in both Biomedicum 1, and Haartman institute. Members of the lab will often(/daily) go to these for lunch.

chunk_id: Work-related guidelines/Working remotely.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: Work-related guidelines/Working remotely.docx
text:
For the lab community and to foster collaborations, on-site work is the default/preferred.
However, if you have a task requiring uninterrupted focusing, or have other compelling reasons you can occasionally work remotely 1-2 days at a time. 
For remote working
1) Inform the Lab for optimal collaboration
Mark your remote day to the lab calendar on the previous day - so that others can adjust their work and meetings accordingly.
For meetings - please notify others in Slack that you will be attending remotely and generate and send the zoom link on the previous day.
If you are working remotely for longer periods e.g. a full week - book at least one update meeting per week with the team/supervisor. 
2) Maintain your working ergonomics
Make sure you have the necessary equipment (screens, keyboards) and working environment also present at your remote working site. These are tax deductible. (cannot be purchased via the Lab). 
Remember to eat and keep regular rhythm and breaks; take a walk outside, socialise, talk to somebody even via zoom.
